modules.topic_name: 1.1 Introduction - Concept Sprint
modules.explanation: Think of this module as the warm-up lap. In 'Introduction', you connect the textbook idea from Chapter 1 to a clear solving habit. A square matrix has an inverse only when its determinant is non-zero. Elementary row or column transformations preserve equivalence while simplifying a matrix. Treat each formula like a tool: first notice when it applies, then use it carefully, and finally check whether the answer fits the condition.

questions.question_text: In Chapter 1 (Applications of Matrices and Determinants), what is the key idea used in the section 'Introduction'?
questions.rationale: A square matrix has an inverse only when its determinant is non-zero.

questions.question_text: Which textbook section number is assigned to 'Introduction' in Class 12 Mathematics?
questions.rationale: The table of contents lists 'Introduction' as section 1.1.

questions.question_text: 'Introduction' belongs to which Class 12 Mathematics chapter?
questions.rationale: The PDF table of contents places 'Introduction' under Chapter 1: Applications of Matrices and Determinants.

questions.question_text: When solving a board-style problem from 'Introduction', which habit best matches the textbook approach?
options: changing the topic into an unrelated formula
identify the relevant definition or theorem first
questions.correct_answer: identify the relevant definition or theorem first
questions.rationale: The textbook builds each topic from definitions, formulae, worked examples, and then exercises.

questions.question_text: Which statement is most accurate for the concept used in 'Introduction'?
options: A square matrix has an inverse only when its determinant is non-zero.
Matrix methods convert simultaneous linear equations into an organized algebra table.
The inverse matrix method solves AX = B by writing X = A inverse B.
Elementary row or column transformations preserve equivalence while simplifying a matrix.
questions.correct_answer: The inverse matrix method solves AX = B by writing X = A inverse B.
questions.rationale: The inverse matrix method solves AX = B by writing X = A inverse B.

questions.question_text: The section 'Introduction' appears in which textbook volume and month plan?
questions.rationale: The textbook contents place Chapter 1 in Volume 1 for June.

modules.topic_name: 8.1 Introduction - Concept Sprint
modules.explanation: Think of this module as the warm-up lap. In 'Introduction', you connect the textbook idea from Chapter 8 to a clear solving habit. Differentials estimate small changes using derivatives. A function of several variables depends on more than one input. Treat each formula like a tool: first notice when it applies, then use it carefully, and finally check whether the answer fits the condition.

questions.question_text: In Chapter 8 (Differentials and Partial Derivatives), what is the key idea used in the section 'Introduction'?

options: changing the topic into an unrelated formula

questions.correct_answer: identify the relevant definition or theorem first
questions.rationale: The textbook builds each topic from definitions, formulae, worked examples, and then exercises.

questions.question_text: Which statement is most accurate for the concept used in 'Introduction'?
options: Differentials estimate small changes using derivatives.
Limits and continuity in two variables depend on behaviour near a point from all paths.
Linear approximation replaces a complicated surface locally with a tangent plane.
A function of several variables depends on more than one input.
questions.correct_answer: Linear approximation replaces a complicated surface locally with a tangent plane.
questions.rationale: Linear approximation replaces a complicated surface locally with a tangent plane.

questions.question_text: The section 'Introduction' appears in which textbook volume and month plan?
questions.rationale: The textbook contents place Chapter 8 in Volume 2 for October/November.
